data.title: Novo Nordisk: The Real Comeback Starts Now With The Wegovy Pill
data.description:
Novo Nordisk: Has The Revival Started With The Wegovy Pill?

A timely "intervention"... this time from the FDA, as Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) (NONOF) looks to arrest the decline that saw the stock getting a much welcome boost in the battle to reshape the obesity drugs race in its favor once again. In this case, Novo Nordisk has finally gotten the approval for the Wegovy pill, allowing the company to potentially put forth a stronger challenge in aiming to reverse the decline that saw the stock give up another 40% over the past year.

Losing market share to Eli Lilly (LLY) has been a really bitter pill to swallow, in which the market also took it as a cue to hammer NVO massively, sending the stock down to multi-year lows, even though the momentum has been consolidating rather positively above the $50 level in the past few months.

In my last Novo update, I noted that the market will likely reset its expectations in view of it losing the Metsera bidding war to Pfizer back then. With the Wegovy pill approval, while it may not represent a sizable M&A deal that could help uplift the market's confidence in its medium- to long-term game against Lilly, I believe it still affords us a timely opportunity to assess whether it could finally help Novo stem the market share losses to Lilly from 2026.

No doubt, Lilly also has its own approvals for Orforglipron which is slated to plausibly be approved by early 2026, rendering any advantage that NVO has earned as somewhat short-lived. So, it may not necessarily provide a prolonged relief beyond the brief respite that NVO hopes to muster. Nevertheless, it's still a notable onward opportunity for Novo to showcase to the market that it still has the wherewithal and capabilities to stage first mover maneuvers in an attempt to wrestle the narrative back again quickly when needed.

The positive sentiments surrounding the Wegovy pill is palpable, and I believe the solid feedback observed in NVO's market action (outperforming the market since my last update) augurs well for the next-gen offerings that NVO and LLY have lined up. Results for Orforglipron could help shift the market's confidence back in favor of NVO for now, but when ascertaining the prospects of a longer-term rebalancing of the Danish headquartered company, it may really require the company to validate by stemming share losses and curbing LLY's superior growth rates. A series of supply chain snafu affecting Novo, while contrasting that with solid execution by Lilly have given them the upper hand to the American biopharma giant in this battle royale, and one that NVO will be remiss to afford another chance to its arch rival once more as the battle moves into the oral phase for these two world beaters in the obesity drugs game.

Novo: The Battle With Lilly Is Still Expected To Be Intense

Nevertheless, the fact is that Wall Street has turned so downcast on Novo's opportunity set that even the unveiling and approval of the Wegovy pill may not necessarily sustain the improvement in its sentiments. For a stock that used to post record-beating growth rates against its healthcare peers, Wall Street is seeing the possibility of a rare decline on a full-year basis for Novo's revenue growth rate. In other words, we might have to wait for another year (in 2027) to reassess whether NVO could emerge from the doldrums, as investors bake in the possibility of seeing more intense competition following through against the formidable Lilly, while also taking into account structural changes in the value chain.

We already know that pricing will no longer be a tailwind to bolster NVO's business, as supply has normalized, competition has heightened, while also facing pressure (from both the market and the government) to lower the prices for the obesity drugs. In the case of Novo, the pressure is even more telling, as LLY is also setting the pace to try and accelerate its market share gains, while fending off a resurgent NVO.

Despite that, I think it's also reasonable for me to surmise that the market should at least feel more assured that NVO, under the new management team, are finally getting their act together. Getting the approvals done quickly and seamlessly with less fuss, securing a period where they could put the Wegovy pill out there and try to incite some panic against LLY while they try and expedite their own approvals for their next gen pipeline. For now, it's going to be quite challenging for us to validate whether NVO can actually stem further share losses, much less think about overturning them.

NVO: Valuations Still Close To 10 Year Lows

Yet, that's the point, isn't it? If the market was already so confident that NVO is surely on its way back up to retake new highs soon, do we really think that the stock will still trade at the 10Y low levels of 10.5x that it's being valued now on a forward EBITDA basis? Come on, let's be real, yeah?

As compared to LLY's forward EBITDA multiple of 24.7x, I believe the market is clearly still sounding the clarion call on LLY's opportunity, while communicating the death knell on NVO's thesis in contrast. But, that's exactly why I believe NVO's extreme pessimism has baked in such deep struggles that it's hard to envisage how much worse it could still get, especially since the stock has been consolidating close to the $50 zone in the past few months.

I believe it augurs well that buyers are firmly accumulating, and think that as the obesity drugs race move to the oral phase (where they could take an estimated 25% of the market according to Leerink Partners, as we welcome the arrival of the next-gen drugs, NVO's first mover advantage here might just prove opportune and timely yet again, as it fires the first salvo in its bid to regain composure and also the lost leadership against its American rival.

While the outcome is definitely too early for us to conclude right now, I think the stage is still set for NVO to (at least) prove its ability to execute and close the valuation bifurcation between them through 2026, plausibly laying a viable pathway for the stock to outperform again.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2026 Bloomberg Watch List Has 4 Ideal "Safer" DiviDog Buys
data.description:
Foreword

This article is from analysts at Bloomberg Intelligence who track 2,000 companies in industries from apparel and autos to finance and food as reported in the December, 2025 Bloomberg Businessweek’s: 50 Companies to Watch.

How Bloomberg analysts collated their list

"The analysis combines contrarian views and catalysts for change such as new leadership, asset sales or acquisitions, and plans for products and services. When building the list, BI also focused on the potential effects of shifting US tariff policies, the race to lock down supplies of vital resources and the ongoing geopolitical conflicts and the transformative effects of artificial intelligence.”—Tim Craighead, Bloomberg Intelligence

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 50 watchable firms are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here, December 17 data focused on 27 dividend payers. The full list of 50 is posted in the Afterword text of this article.

Happily,14 of the 27 dividend-paying watchable companies (Dividend Focus Group) live up to the Dogcatcher “safer’ qualification showing free cash flow yields exceeding dividend yields. As of 12/17/25, they were: Marvell Technologies, Inc. (MRVL), Electronic Arts Inc. (EA), Capitec Bank Holdings Limited (CKHGY), Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF), Emerson Electric Co. (EMR), CF Industries Holdings, Inc. (CF), OTP Bank Nyrt. (OTPBF), ACS, Actividades de Construcción y Servicios, S.A. (ACSAF), Aviva plc (AIVAF), Sodexo S.A. (SDXAY), Danske Bank A/S(DNKEY), and Western Midstream Partners, LP Common Units (WES). Many first-time investors regard this condition as an invitation to look-closer.

The fourteen “safer” dividends showed free cash flow yield exceeding their dividend yield. Of those fourteen, four IDEAL also paid dividends from $1 thousand invested greater than their single share price. The IDEAL 4 were: Aviva ; Sodexo ; Danske Bank; Western Midstream. Many first time buyers take this IDEAL signal as a “watch to Buy” sign.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 13.42% To 37.98% Net Gains From Ten Top 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus Companies To December, 2026

Three of ten top 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus company stocks by yield were among the top ten gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, the yield-based forecast for these 2026 Bloomberg top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 30% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, supplied the data points. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable top yielders as of 12/17/25 were:

Bloomberg Top Ten September Dividend Focus 2025 Dogs By Net Gains

Marvell Technology was projected to net $379.79 based on the median of target prices from 44 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 93% over the market as a whole.

Nike, Inc. (NKE) was projected to net $252.52 based on the median of target estimates from 40 analysts, plus its last dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% over the market as a whole.

Perusahaan Perseroan (Persero) PT Telekomunikasi Indonesia Tbk (TLK) was projected to net $227.07, based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 60% less than the market as a whole.

Diageo plc (DEO) was projected to net $216.52, based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 7 brokers. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 45% less than the market as a whole.

Agilent Technologies, Inc. (A) was projected to net $206.47 based on a median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% over the market as a whole.

Taiwan Semiconductor was projected to net $204.74 based on on the median of target prices from 17 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 31% more than the market as a whole.

CF Industries was projected to net $200.83 based on dividends, plus the median of the target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 32% less than the market as a whole.

Western Midstream was projected to net $161.13 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 14 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 24% less than the market as a whole.

Voya Financial, Inc. (VOYA) was projected to net $142.51 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% under the market as a whole.

Emerson Electric was projected to net $134.23, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 29 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 26% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 212.58% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 3% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

25 of 47 Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show December Positive Annual Returns (22 More Show Losses)

26 of 47 Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show December Analyst Target Gains (3 Show Losses; 18 Are Unrated)

27 of 47 Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show Positive December Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top Bloomberg Dividend Focus Dogs By Yield For November

Top yield ten December Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks represented seven of eleven Morningstar sectors.

One energy stock placed first, Western Midstream [1], while three financial services stocks occupied the second, sixth and ninth places, Danske Bank [2], Aviva [6], and Commonwealth bank of Australia (CMWAX) [9].

Then one communications stock placed third, PT Telecom Indonesia [3], while two consumer discretionary stocks occupied the fourth and tenth places, Sodexo SA [4] and Continental Aktiengesellschaft (CTTAY) [10].

One consumer staples sector member placed fifth, Diageo [5]. Then a industrials representative placed seventh, ACS Actividades de Construction [7]. Finally, another loner, a real estate sector stock, placed eighth, Keppel DC REIT (KPDCF) [8] to complete the top-ten December list of Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks by yield.

One Bloomberg Dividend Focus Dog made the top 16 by Yield, Analyst Targets, and Total Returns

Bloomberg Dividend Focus 2026 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 12/17/25-6

The above chart reveals 27 Bloomberg Dividend Focus dogs out of 48 subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by its annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive divi-dog on the list is Western Midstream at $10.66 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is Marvell Technology at $350.26 per dividend dollar.

Five of Twenty-seven December Dividend Paying Bloomberg 2026 Focus Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Five dividend paying Bloomberg stocks so infected are: Diageo, Keppel DC REIT, Commonwealth Bank of Australia, Nucor, and Constellation Energy. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, trade deals, wars, ICE raids and tacoriffs, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top-Yield 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus Stocks Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, seven in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those already in the ideal zone, three more top-yield stocks are within 45% of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that Diageo, Asahi Group Holdings, Ltd. (ACSAF), and Carlsberg A/S (CMWAY) raise their dividend or split shares to conform to the Dogcatcher ideal. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Just watch, then buy, when your targeted stock-price hits the sweet spot. Give it a couple of months.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr Targets Showed 10.98% To 38.69% Top Ten 2026 Bloomberg Upsides To December, 2026; (32) Two -0.88% to - 6.39% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 18.82% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Bloomberg 2026 Focus Stocks to December, 2026

Ten-top Focus Stocks were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus stocks selected 12/17/25 by yield, represented seven of eleven sectors. health care, materials, technology, and utilities, missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expect 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Bloomberg 2026 Stocks (34) To Deliver 6.94% Vs. (35) 8.55% Net Gains by All Ten Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten 2026 Dividend Focus analyst 1-year targets to deliver 18.82% LESS gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fifth lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, PT Telekom, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 22.71%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield 2026 Dividend Focus dogs for December were: Keppel DC REIT;Continental AG; Aviva; Sodexo; PT Telekom, with prices ranging from $1.77 to $21.33.

Five higher-priced 2026 Dividend Focus top-yield dogs as of December 17 were: Danske Bank; Western Midstream; Diageo; ACS Actividades, Commonwealth Bank of Australia, with prices ranging $24.24 to $102.81.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As euphoria of the holiday market cools and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into early 2026 with the exception of a few meme stocks, look for most of the top ten 2026 Bloomberg Focus Dividend Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 4 Ideal of 14 ‘Safer' Bloomberg 2025 Focus Stocks

Mid-December 2025, 14 of the 47 Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

YCharts 12/17/25 data showed that four of fourteen IDEAL dividends (from $1,000 invested) exceeding single share price, were also “Safer”: Western Midstream, Danske Bank, Sodexo, and Aviva.

How 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Data Produced "Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that four dividends from $1,000.00 invested in our top-ten 'safer' dividend focus stocks conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those four ideal dogs were Western Midstream, Danske Bank, Sodexo, and Aviva.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

A market correction of only 65% could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or split shares to the dogcatcher ideal proportions. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Footnotes

The Full List of Bloomberg 50 Companies to Watch in 2026

Three stocks on the original Bloomberg 50 list were unavailable through YCharts sources. The listing above shows that one Chinese Healthcare stock, one overseas bank and an co-op Insurance firm were not listed in YCharts, two were probably private enterprises.

The net-gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer” Bloomberg Watchlist dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

data.title: Tech Will Deliver In 2026: AI, Quantum Computing To Propel Stocks Higher
data.description:
2025 was a fascinating year. From the massive Black Swan drop in April to the remarkable rally that followed one of the steepest declines in stock market history. While we've seen a bit of unpredictable price action in 2025, 2026 also appears like it could be a very exciting year.

However, not all sectors are created equally, and some segments of the market are likelier to outperform than others. Therefore, in this article, I will focus on which sectors could do well, some could do OK, and others may even struggle in the new year.

1. (XLB) State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF - The materials segment could do very well. The XLB ETF includes several top companies I like, including Newmont (NEM), Freeport (FCX), Linde plc (LIN), and other material industry giants. I expect these and other high-quality companies in the materials space to do well in 2026, and I give this sector a score of 7.8 out of 10, given all the variables, suggesting there should be numerous solid buys in this space.

2. (XLC) State Street Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLC is a tech heavy ETF that I believe can do well next year. Several of the standout companies in this ETF include Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), which account for roughly 25% of this ETF's weight.

I generally believe that tech could have another solid year, including segments like communication services, and AI. I give the communication services segment a 7.9 out of 10 score for next year, implying there should be plenty of excellent buying opportunities here in 2026.

3. (XLE) State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLE is a solid energy ETF. However, its top two holdings Exxon (XOM) and Chevron (CVX) account for approximately 40% of this ETF's weight. Generally, I like energy, oil, and gas going into 2026. Oil remains extremely cheap here while it's still below $60, and there's a high probability of oil moving higher in the new year as inflation, demand, and monetary easing intensify. I rate this segment at a 7.8, giving it solid upside potential next year.

4. (XLF) State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLF is a solid financials ETF. While some financials, especially in fintech and digital asset platforms bridging traditional banking, could do well in 2026, other, more traditional banks may face some challenges due to lower rates.

However, despite lower rates, top banks could make up for the shortfalls in lending in other areas. Still, I'm neutral on this space, assigning it a 7.5 rating, essentially on par with a hold.

5. (XLI) State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLI is similar to the materials ETF in some respects, as some segments may be intertwined. Still, XLI is heavy in GE components, RTX Corp. (RTX), Boeing (BA), and other high-quality industrial names. Due to the potential boom in commodities, increasing inflation, and easier monetary policy, XLI will likely do well as we move forward in 2026. I rate it a 7.7 here, illustrating a buy rating for this space.

6. (XLK) State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLK is one of my favorite ETFs as it represents the tech space. XLK is heavy in Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), AMD (AMD), and other tech heavyweights. I believe the tech sector is mostly set up well for 2026, and AI could continue driving growth in the tech space going forward.

Moreover, we're seeing many AI subsections, quantum computing, and other segments beginning to break out, implying more growth ahead in 2026. Given the easier monetary environment and the potential for higher than expected growth, the tech sector could do very well, and I rate it an 8.2, illustrating a strong buy rating going into 2026.

7. (XLP) State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR - I'm not a big investor in the consumer staples space. However, the XLP ETF has several solid companies, including Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and others. This sector may also outperform if the economy goes through a mild slowdown or if there's more rotation toward risk averse equities in stocks. Still, many stocks here already trade at premium valuations. Therefore, I give the consumer staples segment a 7.4 here, implying a hold rating into 2024.

8. (XLRE) The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF - I prefer not to invest in REITs at all. Still, with interest rates likely to fall, the REIT segment could do well. I give this space a 7.5 score, implying a hold rating in 2026.

9. (XLU) State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF - I also don't invest that much in utilities, unless it's the top nuclear names. XLU has some exposure to Vistra (VST) and others. However, for nuclear-specific exposure, I prefer the (NLR) VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF. I give general utilities a 7.5 hold score rating here. However, the nuclear segment and the SMR space in particular get a 7.9, solid buy rating score here, in my view.

10. (XLV) State Street Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF - I'm neutral to mildly bullish on healthcare and biotech. The industry faces headwinds. However, at the same time, it's had a rough start in 2025 and may still be oversold and undervalued in many regards. AI could also provide increased growth and efficiency in this segment. XLV provides solid exposure to some high-quality companies in the space, and I rate the healthcare space a 7.7 here, suggesting a buy rating for this space.

11. (XLY) State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF - XLY is an interesting ETF. I think the whole consumer discretionary segment breakdown is fascinating. The two most significant holdings here are Tesla (TSLA) and Amazon (AMZN), weighing roughly 42% of the entire ETF. Therefore, I like XLY a lot here as Tesla remains my most significant holding and is one of the stocks I'm most bullish on. Nonetheless, I would argue that companies like Tesla and Amazon should be in the tech segment ETFs.

In any case, I'm bullish on Tesla, Amazon, and other high-quality tech, but I'm neutral on consumer discretionary, in general. I give the traditional consumer discretionary segment a 7.6 rating, which is the equivalent to a slight buy.

Outlook: Bullish Sectors in 2026

Out of the 11 primary sectors, I still like technology as my No. 1 pick. Moreover, within tech, I would consider several ETFs including the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ), ARK Next Generation Internet ETF (ARKW), and several others.

Another ETF I like a lot is the Global X Lithium & Battery Tech ETF (LIT). iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF (SLVP) may be my favorite gold and silver ETF here, and I expect gold and silver to continue rocking in 2026. Regarding Bitcoin and other digital assets, I prefer to hold Strategy (MSTR) at these depressed levels. However, if we're discussing traditional ETFs, then Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC) and Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHE) are solid options in my view. I also like the Global X Blockchain ETF (BKCH) for Bitcoin miner, exchange, and blockchain infrastructure exposure.

I'm setting my year-end 2026 S&P 500 target at 8,000. I also believe that if the AI narrative doesn't unravel, the Nasdaq could eclipse 30,000 in 2026.

Greatest Risks To Consider

There are risks, of course, despite my relatively bullish assessment. The most significant risk may be the potential slowdown in the AI buildout process, disruptions in circular financing, and other substantial deals. The million dollar question really is, "when will the "AI bubble" pop.

Well, I believe the AI segment is destined to temporarily deflate from time to time. Nonetheless, we likely have more growth potential as there may be more gas in the AI tank. If I ranked the AI market as a baseball game, we may be around the sixth or seventh inning.

Therefore, while we should be more mindful of a likely eventual more significant deflationary phase in the AI segment, it may not arrive in 2026, and possibly not in 2027, as well. It's possible that the market reaches a high point and has a possible recession in 2028. Nonetheless, we must also be mindful of general slowdown risks due to a problematic labor market, elevated inflation, a stretched consumer, and other elements. There are also various uncertainties, including the Fed, the future Fed leader, and the exact path of future monetary policy. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in any of the ETFs mentioned, and stocks in general.

data.title: Celcuity: After Stellar 2025, I Predict More Success In 2026 And Beyond
data.description:
Investment Overview

This is my first time initiating coverage of Celcuity Inc. (CELC) for Seeking Alpha. The Minneapolis based biotech raised a modest $26.2m in its September 2018 IPO, at a share price of $9.5, and it's lead therapeutic candidate today is - according to the company's Q3 2025 10Q filing:

gedatolisib, a kinase inhibitor of the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (“PI3K”), serine/threonine-protein kinase protein kinase B (“AKT”), mechanistic target of rapamycin (“mTOR”) pathway that binds to all Class I PI3K isoforms and the mTOR complexes, mTORC1 and mTORC2. By targeting all Class I PI3K isoforms and mTORC1/2, gedatolisib induces comprehensive inhibition of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR (“PAM”) pathway.

Gedatolisib is being developed to treat patients with HR+/HER2-/PIK3CA wild-type ("WT") advanced breast cancer ("ABC"), and in July, Celcuity's share price exploded, as the company reported data from its Phase 3 VIKTORIA-1 clinical study of gedatolisib plus fulvestrant, with and without palbociclib, versus fulvestrant in patients with HR+/HER2- ABC.

According to the company's Q2 earnings press release:

The efficacy results established several new milestones in the history of drug development for HR+/HER2- ABC.

  • The gedatolisib triplet (gedatolisib, fulvestrant and palbociclib) reduced the risk of disease progression or death by 76% compared to fulvestrant based on a hazard ratio (“HR”) of 0.24. The median PFS was 9.3 months with the gedatolisib triplet versus 2.0 months with fulvestrant, an incremental improvement of 7.3 months.
  • The gedatolisib doublet (gedatolisib and fulvestrant) reduced the risk of disease progression or death by 67% compared to fulvestrant based on a hazard ratio of 0.33. The median PFS was 7.4 months with the gedatolisib doublet versus 2.0 months with fulvestrant, an incremental improvement of 5.4 months.
  • Both gedatolisib regimens showed lower rates of hyperglycemia and stomatitis and the rate of discontinuation of all treatment due to a treatment-related adverse event (“AE”), was lower than was reported in a Phase 1b study in this patient population.

After the results were shared, Celcuity stock climbed from from ~$14 per share, to ~$37 per share. Fulvestrant is marketed and sold by Pharma giant AstraZeneca (AZN), brand name Fasoldex, and palbociclib marketed and sold by Pfizer (PFE), brand name Ibrance - expected revenues in 2025 >$4bn.

The results far exceeded analysts' and even management's expectations, and the company announced at the end of August that the:

U.S. Food and Drug Administration (“FDA”) agreed to accept its New Drug Application (“NDA”) for gedatolisib in HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer for review under the Real-Time Oncology Review (“RTOR”) program, which facilitates earlier submission of topline efficacy and safety results, prior to the submission of the complete application, to support an earlier start to the FDA’s evaluation of the application.

Celcuity stock jumped again in mid-October, as management shared data from a Phase 1 study of gedatolisib in combo with Nubeqa(darolutamide), an androgen receptor inhibitor, in men with metastatic, castration-resistant prostate cancer ("mCRPC") - according to a press release:

The six-month rPFS rate and median rPFS for patients from both arms combined was 67% and 9.1 months, respectively. For patients treated with 120 mg gedatolisib, the six-month rPFS rate was 74% and median rPFS was 9.5 months. For patients treated with 180 mg gedatolisib, the six-month rPFS rate was 61% and the median rPFS was 7.4 months.

By the time Celcuity released its Q3 earnings on November 12th, stock traded ~$85 per share - up >500% since late July - and it has kept climbing, trading >$100 at the time of writing.

In July, Celcuity raised $287m via a public offering of 2.750% convertible senior notes due 2031, common stock and pre-funded warrants, and also announced it had:

entered into an amendment to its existing senior secured term loan facility with an affiliate of Innovatus Capital Partners, LLC (“Innovatus”), and Oxford Finance LLC and certain of its affiliates (together, “Oxford”). The amendment increases the total term loan facility size to $500 million, including $350 million in committed capital and up to $150 million at the mutual discretion of Celcuity and its lenders.

The company reported a net loss of $(43.8m), or $(0.92) per share for the quarter, and $(126.1m) for the year-to-date, or $(2.81) per share. Current assets were reported as $475m, with $74m cash, and $381m of short-term investments.

Analysis - After A Sensational 2025, What Does 2026 Hold In Store For Celcuity?

Understanding why the gedatolisib data has resulted in such stratospheric gains for Celcuity stock is not straightforward, given the many different types of breast cancer, and the different ways it can be treated with a wide variety of drugs, with different mechanisms of action ("MoAs").

As we can see above, the PAM pathway is an oncogenic driver in a large percentage of cancers, and could address a patient population of >500k, Celcuity believes, which is one significant reasons why study results have been greeted with such enthusiasm by the market - potentially, gedatolisib may one day secure approvals in multiple different lines in prostate, and breast cancers.

A second reason is that the PAM pathway can be difficult to target, with safety an issue for previous drug candidates, but with its differentiated MoA, as shown below, gedatolisib achieves greater potency than rival drugs, such as Novartis' (NVS) Piqray (alpelisib), estimated 2025 revenues of ~$400m, or AstraZeneca's Truqap (capivasertib), estimated revenues >$600m in 2025.

Gedatolisib's safety data to date also seems to set it apart from current standards of care ("SoCs"), as shown below in this comparison of safety in the VIKTORIA-1 study.

From an efficacy perspective, gedatolisib also appears to be an improvement on SoCs, with the VIKTORIA progression free survival ("PFS") figure of 9.3 months superior to, e.g., Novartis' Afinitor (everolimus), Eli Lilly's (LLY) Inluriyo (imlunestrant) and Orserdu, mareketed and sold by Stemline Therapeutics. The latest data shared by Celcuity on December 11th, revealed that:

For patients whose time to progression on immediate prior therapy was >18 months, median progression-free survival (“PFS”) was 12.4 months with gedatolisib + palbociclib + fulvestrant (“gedatolisib triplet”) and 10.0 months with gedatolisib + fulvestrant (“gedatolisib doublet”) versus 1.9 months for fulvestrant

The data certainly seems to set up an approval in 2026 for gedatolisib in second line breast cancer treatment, and on its Q3 earnings call with analysts, Brian Sullivan, co-founder and Chairman of Celcuity told analysts:

we believe it's reasonable to estimate that a second-line wild-type indication or second-line indication in general for gedatolisib can potentially generate peak revenues of $2.5 billion to $3 billion.

Given Celcuity's current market cap is $4.7bn, if we agree management's peak revenue figure - which does not feel too excessive, given, e.g., Novartis' Kisqali and Pfizer's Ibrance will earn >$4bn revenues this year, and applying a "rule-of-thumb" that a commercial stage Pharma often trades at 3/4x sales, we may speculate Celcuity's market cap could easily double once gedatolisib is approved and a commercial launch executed.

Furthermore, we should also note the Phase 3 VIKTORIA-2 study in first line ABC is ongoing, with earlier study data showing a median PFS of 48.6 months, and objective response rate ("ORR") of 79% - significantly better than current SoCs - and the successful early stage prostate readout. Another catalyst to be aware of comes in the first half of next year, as management reports:

The PIK3CA mutant cohort of the Phase 3 VIKTORIA-1 clinical trial is fully enrolled with topline data expected sometime in late Q1 2026 or during Q2 2026.

In short, then, to answer my question re 2026, gedatolisib appears to be a drug the market ought to be excited about, because it has - to date - demonstrated the credentials of a best-in-class drug in waiting, and although there is unlikely to be an approval in 2026, as the NDA will not be fully submitted until late 2026, I am struggling to find reasons why approval would not be granted, or why the drug could not swiftly achieve "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) status, and continue to grow.

Concluding Thoughts: A 750% Six-Month Share Price Gain Is Mind Boggling - But I See More Upside Ahead

There are a few caveats to my buy thesis - firstly, cross trial comparisons can be unreliable, and in a real world setting, physicians and patients may continue to favor other therapies. Secondly, competition is fierce and multiple Pharmas and biotechs are busy developing new drug targeting the breast cancer space, and thirdly, rivals may be better resourced in terms of marketing, infrastructure, and finances. Celcuity may boast nearly $500m cash, but I'd speculate that funding could be used up by the end of 2027, as regulatory submissions, and commercial launches are capital intensive.

Nevertheless, there is nothing better in biotech circles than plenty of positive data supporting a drug's best-in-class safety and efficacy, and scientific validation of a unique mechanism of action, which Celcuity appears to have in relation to gedatolisib.

There is always a chance that the PIK3CA mutant cohort data underwhelms, and undermines the goodwill towards gedatolisib in the markets, but there is no compelling reason to believe that will be the case, in my view, and I'd expect to see a favorable set of data that may be further value enhancing.

I doubt that Celcuity's share price and valuation will continue to witness the explosive upside of 2025, in 2026, but steady progress towards the goal of a first commercial approval ought to keep stock buoyant, in my view, and if the drug does make it to market in 2027, and revenue numbers impress, as I expect they might, then by 2028, I certainly think market cap could trend in the $7.5bn - $10bn region. We should also consider the prospect of M&A also - Pharmas may be prepared to pay a substantial sum - >$10bn perhaps, to acquire a drug that could enjoy 5-10 years of "blockbuster" sales.

All things considered then, and despite the gains made in 2025, I'd make Celcuity a fairly solid long-term buy and hold opportunity.

data.title: Realty Income Remains A Compelling Prospect
data.description:
Back in August of this year, I made the decision to upgrade Realty Income Corporation (O) from a "Hold" back to a "Buy." The company has continued to demonstrate high quality. Compared to most other similar REITs, leverage is low, and its yield is high. Valuation is a bit mixed, to be honest. But at the end of the day, for an industry leader that continues to expand nicely, some premium probably is justified. The recent data provided by management shows continued strong growth, both on the top and bottom lines. And long term, that trend is likely to continue. Unfortunately, the stock has underperformed the market recently, rising only 3.1% since that article was published. This compares to the 7% increase that the S&P 500 experienced over the same window of time. But as disappointing as that is to see, the good news is that it offers investors an opportunity to still buy in at reasonable levels.

Realty Income Offers More Upside

The world of REITs is vast. But among all the publicly traded ones that exist out there, one of the largest is Realty Income. The firm boasts a market capitalization of $52.63 billion. And while it could be said that it is diverse in nature, it does have certain areas of concentration. For instance, about 82.3% of its annualized base rent comes from the United States. Another 13.9% comes from the UK, while the remaining 3.8% comes from continental Europe. So this is very much a U.S.-based firm.

Furthermore, it is an enterprise focused primarily on owning and leasing out retail assets. As of this writing, 79.8% of its annualized base rent comes from retail. Industrial is a distant second at 14.7%. We have gaming at 3.1%. And all other miscellaneous property types comprise the remaining 2.4%. Retail is not something that traditionally appeals to me. But this is not just shopping centers, malls, grocery stores, and the like. Yes, the company does have a lot of exposure to the grocery space, with 10.8% of annualized base rent coming from that category. Convenience stores are a close second at 9.7%. But the firm also has meaningful exposure to home improvement stores, dollar stores, quick-service restaurants, health and fitness centers, drug stores, automotive service centers, casual dining restaurants, and general merchandise.

The good news is that none of its clients are terribly material to it. Its largest exposure happens to be to gas station operator 7-Eleven, which is responsible for 3.3% of its annualized base rent. Other major tenants include Dollar General (DG), Walgreens, Family Dollar, and the like. All combined, its top 20 largest clients make up just 36.3% of its annualized base rent. This means that anyone tenant folding would not have a huge impact on the top or bottom lines of the business.

Over the years, management has maintained a high-quality portfolio of assets. The company invests heavily in purchasing new properties, but is not afraid to sell off ones that no longer make sense to own. This refresh cycle has permitted it to maintain high occupancy rates. In fact, the average from the last 25 years has been 98.3%. And as of the third quarter of this year, the 98.7% rating that we get is the second-highest reading that we have seen this century. Only the 99% reading that was experienced back in 2022 is higher than what the company has right now. Very obviously, management is doing a fine job here.

Management tries to lock in long-term tenants. This is evidenced by the fact that the weighted average lease term right now is about 8.9 years. And from this year through the end of this decade, tenants representing only 35.9% of annualized base rent are expected to see their leases expire. The good news is that the company has a great track record of re-leasing its assets and has historically done so in a way that has ultimately boosted revenue moving forward.

I mentioned earlier that management has a track record of refreshing the company's portfolio. So far this year, for instance, the company has made investments amounting to $3.9 billion. This was based on sourced volume of $97 billion, which is the highest in the company's history. For context, for all of 2024, investment volume was just $3.9 billion. But the year before that, it amounted to an astounding $9.5 billion. Europe appears to be particularly interesting to the company. Management describes Europe as a fragmented competitive landscape that offers a favorable cost of capital.

This is why the company has invested almost $15 billion into the continent since it began its international expansion efforts in 2019. $2.8 billion of this investment has occurred so far this year. But of course, this does not mean that the company is neglecting opportunities here. On Dec. 1, management announced that it entered into an agreement to make an $800 million perpetual preferred equity investment in the real estate of CityCenter, which involves a resort and casino, as well as a hotel and spa. This move alone caused management to increase its overall investment volume this year to over $6 billion. This compares to the $5.5 billion that the company was planning to make when it announced results for the third quarter of its 2025 fiscal year. And even that was up from the $5 billion reported when I last wrote about the company.

Fundamentally speaking, Realty Income has done a fine job as of late. For the most recent quarter, which would be the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue came in at $1.47 billion. That's a nice increase over the $1.33 billion the company reported a year earlier. Obviously, re-leasing activities and rent escalators helped here. But a big driver has always been an expansion of its physical footprint. The company currently has 15,542 properties in its portfolio. This compares to the 15,457 that it had at the same time last year. Clearly, things are going well.

As revenue increased, profitability for the institution grew also. Net income is not terribly important in this space. But year over year, it grew from $261.8 million to $315.8 million. Other profitability metrics grew also. We saw operating cash flow jump from $841.5 million to $943.1 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $808.1 million to $885.1 million. FFO, or funds from operations, grew from $854.9 million to $981.1 million. And on an adjusted basis, we would get an increase from $915.6 million to $992 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.23 billion to $1.34 billion.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows have all been rising nicely. But this is nothing new for the company. In my prior articles about it, I have detailed how stable and consistent the growth for this enterprise has been. And that is quite common in this space. What's more, we can likely expect this trend to continue in the near term.

For this year, management expects adjusted FFO per share of between $4.25 and $4.27. At the midpoint, we would be looking at adjusted FFO of about $3.90 billion. That compares to the $3.62 billion the company reported a year earlier. In the chart above, you can see what I estimated for other key profitability metrics and how those stacked up against what the company achieved for the 2024 fiscal year. Clearly, the company is on an uptrend.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Frankly, these are the kinds of multiples that I would typically associate with a fair value prospect. But in this space, especially with a high-quality operator that is an industry leader, I do provide additional wiggle room. And in fact, relative to other similar firms, the stock certainly doesn't look expensive. In the chart below, I used two of its metrics to compare against five such enterprises. On a price-to-operating cash flow basis, four of the five were cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only one of these enterprises ended up being cheaper than it is.

There are other things that we should consider here when evaluating an investment opportunity. For starters, leverage is incredibly important. The sad truth is that many REITs have a tendency to take on too much leverage because their entire corporate structure is organized to prioritize not only growth, but also significant distributions to shareholders. Fortunately, leverage is in check. In the chart below, I compared it to the same five REITs that I looked at already. Two of them have leverage that is lower than it does, while the other three are higher.

This in-check leverage makes the yield component even more impressive. Currently, Realty Income has a yield of 5.67%. That alone is pretty hefty. And relative to other similar enterprises, it is high. In the chart below, you can see that four of these five competitors have yields that are lower than what it has. This means that, in addition to being decently priced and relatively low in terms of leverage, the business also has proven to be successful in paying out a disproportionate share of capital towards shareholders directly. This is great to see for those who like to chase high payouts.

Takeaway

When you add all of these items together, it's difficult not to be at least slightly bullish about Realty Income. The company seems to be doing a fine job when it comes to growth. That trend should probably continue, especially as management targets key growth opportunities like those in Europe. The business seems stable, especially when you factor in its overall leverage. And with a high payout, I believe that it justifies a soft "Buy" rating.

data.title: Fiserv: Avoiding Falling Knives
data.description:
Fiserv, Inc. (FISV) plummeted by almost 70% in 2025. This massive dip is explained by repeated earnings disappointments, slashed guidance, and the class-action lawsuit. Wall Street analysts believe that FISV is substantially undervalued, with a projected 36% potential upside. However, I prefer to stay away because the forward P/E ratio setup is weak, with no contraction expected over the next several years. Moreover, the 2023-2025 guidance roller-coaster together with earnings disappointment suggests that the management lacks ability to reliably forecast the company's financial performance.

Last but not least, there is a class-action lawsuit initiated by some of the company's investors, which means significant legal and reputational risks. Overall, I prefer to stay away from this falling knife and assign a Hold rating for FISV.

Fundamental Analysis

Fiserv provides information management and e-commerce systems for financial services companies. Among Fiserv's services are online bill payment, account processing, account management, mobile banking, and debit card transaction processing. There are two segments: Merchant Solutions and Financial Solutions, each generating approximately the same amount of revenue. According to the latest 10-Q form, approximately 84% of revenue is generated domestically, with the remainder generated internationally with a relatively wide international presence.

If we look at Fiserv's share price chart over the last five years, we can see that there was a big bull run from around $120 in late 2023 to almost $240 by February 2025. This share price success was explained by strong organic growth, margin expansion, and bullish guidance revisions. However, expectations grew too fast because the company consistently failed to deliver positive revenue surprises over several quarters since late 2023. The biggest problem was the Q3 2025 earnings release, when FISV delivered a large negative revenue surprise together with a disastrous EPS miss. It was actual EPS of $2.04 against $2.65 consensus forecast. The stock plunged from $126 closer to $60 after earnings, and this reaction was explained not only by a wide EPS miss but also by a significant guidance cut.

Fiserv's guidance for 2023-2024 initially indicated impressive growth, followed by a notable divergence in actual performance, and ultimately a significant cut in guidance. I am not making any allegations, but these sharp changes of the management's guidance are difficult to reconcile with the macroeconomic reality. Yes, there is vast macroeconomic uncertainty due to high-interest rates and increased tariffs. Fiserv's business depends on the economy's health because total payment volumes drive the company's earnings.

However, the uncertainty does not equal sharp changes in the U.S. economy's growth pace. The country's GDP is still growing confidently, while unemployment rate remains notably below long-term historical averages. Once again, I am not making any allegations. However, the fact that some FISV investors initiated class action lawsuit against Fiserv and certain managers. This shows that investors are really concerned, which could make it a long and costly legal battle. A class-action lawsuit is always a big risk because it can cause severe reputational and financial damage. This is the big reason why I do not want to invest in FISV.

Apart from substantial legal risks and long record of disappointing earnings, I also prefer to avoid Fiserv because the digital payments industry demonstrates weakness. This is evident from the recent downgrade from J.P. Morgan for both FISV and PayPal (PYPL), citing "slower market growth and fears of commoditization." Moreover, the U.S. economic growth is poised to slow down in 2026 as the unemployment rate is steadily climbing while the Fed remains in the "no rush mode" regarding interest rate cuts, which is a notable macro headwind for Fiserv and companies alike.

Valuation Analysis

As a result of the massive 2025 share price drop, Fiserv is currently a $36 billion company. Most of the valuation ratios are extremely low compared to FISV's historical averages and the sector median. On the other hand, low multiples are likely explained by the fact that with all the aforementioned challenges, Fiserv's forward P/E ratio is expected to remain stable-as-table over the next several years. It implies that there will be no significant changes in the company's EPS, meaning no bottom-line growth.

Besides no projected EPS growth at the moment, let's not forget FISV's weak earnings surprise performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results, but it significantly affects probability of future outperformance or underperformance. With a poor earnings surprise record, there are high chances that we will see EPS underperformance in the future as well. It is another bearish factor weighing on the P/E contraction potential.

Overall, valuation analysis demonstrates mixed outcomes. I think that currently low multiples are fair given the class-action lawsuit and no projected P/E ratio contraction for several years ahead.

Mitigating Factors

While I believe that FISV is cheap for a reason, Wall Street analysts tend to disagree with me. The average price target is $92, which implies a compelling 36% upside potential. I could have ignored consensus target price if FISV was a small, under-the-radar company. However, FISV is an S&P 500 company, meaning that it is extensively covered by dozens of Wall Street analysts. The fact that their average price target suggests massive upside might indicate that I am overestimating the legal risk or adverse effect on FISV's growth potential from the increasing unemployment rate in the U.S.

Moreover, for a company with a large customer base and industry-leading profitability, there is always probability of a rapid comeback. The aforementioned class-action lawsuit is a risk, but Fiserv's reputation appears to be still strong because the company recently teamed up with Visa (V) to advance agentic commerce. With its $675 billion market cap, Visa is the largest company in this industry, and partnership with this giant might offset Fiserv's reputational damage from the class-action lawsuit.

Conclusion

Without exaggeration, FISV is a falling knife as the stock lost 70% of its value in 2025, which is extremely rare for an S&P 500 company. I usually tend to stay away from falling knives because such massive sell-offs rarely occur with no fundamental reason. The class-action lawsuit is a big risk, and FISV's weak earnings record is another big reason to be cautious. Fiserv has its strong sides and advantages, but I think that positives and negatives offset each other as of today. Therefore, a Hold looks like the most suitable rating for FISV.

data.title: Fenix Gold Offers Deep Value As Rio2 Production Launches Into A Gold Rush
data.description:
Among factors most important to determine whether a gold stock is worthy of investment are: management competency, gold deposit size & longevity, and complexity of mine plan. Rio2 Limited (RIOFF) merits checks across the board. The stock trades at $2.16 per share with a market cap of $868 million. Once production begins in January, the stock should calibrate closer to net present value-(NPV). I calculate a NPV of ~$2 billion or $4.72 stock price using company provided feasibility report, while adjusting to a gold price assumption of $4,300 per ounce.

Management includes executive chairman Alex Black, whose team delivered terrific results with Rio Alto mining which bought and developed a $48 million mine in Peru eventually selling the company to Tahoe Resources for $1.1 billion.

Rio2 holds a lucrative appreciating asset - the 1.7 million ounce Fenix gold mine project located in Chile. There are 3.3 million additional ounces currently listed as measure and indicated for a total of ~5 million. The mine plan projects 91,000 ounces of gold annually for the first 12 years, and 1.3 million total over full life at 17 years.

The oxide ore deposit is among the 3 largest open pit designs currently under development in North & South America. While many open pit mines must grind through vast rock waste, Fenix mine is unique with a very low .85 strip ratio. This factor dramatically reduces operational costs as there is less excavation necessary to generate gold. Similar open pit mines at Marigold and Mesquite, encounter more than 3x waste to produce gold.

Note the gold recovery rate is projected at 75% and the gold price was set at $1,750/ounce in the 2023 study. Two years out, and gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) are now at $4,500.

The mine projects to deliver 91,000 ounces of gold annually over the first 12 years, with cash costs at ~$1,237 per ounce. I chose a conservative gold price held flat at $4,300 per ounce to estimate a net present value. I believe the gold price will rise higher in 2026 based on central bank and investor demand for physical metal amidst declining purchasing power of fiat currencies-the dollar being most important.

Investors should be attracted to potential for significant cash flow that will accelerate in future years as operating costs are likely to remain relatively stable. The ore deposit is excavated, dumped on the leach pad and treated with a solution to produce gold.

Management anticipates a mine expansion that will eventually increase gold production up to ~250,000 ounces per year by increasing tonnage mined from 20,000 to 80,000 per day. This would require increased water supply emanating via a pipeline from a desalination plant. This expansion would likely occur in a few years, and the cost likely to be shared by a neighboring mine operators such as Kinross. Rio2 expects to receive cost estimates in the first quarter.

Additional gold production via expansion would obviously drive this stock price much higher pending the expansion study results. The costs to expand are expected to be moderate considering the favorable dynamics with a heap leach oxide mining operation with an open pit. As more ore is added, the additional production of gold ounces tends to drive cash flow higher than costs. The water pipeline will be key to driving an expanded processing rate and Rio2 is engaged with neighboring mine operators who would benefit from a partnership.

Acquisition of Condestable mine

Rio2 recently announced the purchase the Condestable copper mine from Southern Peaks mining for $241 million in early December.

This acquisition appears to add two significant benefits. Most importantly the mine adds immediate ~$100 million in cash flow with EBITDA projected at $145 million. This copper producing mine will support the expansion costs for both Fenix and Condestable mines. Rio2 will add 80,000 gold equivalent ounces (GEO’s) annually with this transaction expected to close in late January, with an AISC of ~$2,100 per ounce. Free cash flow would likely provide a higher stock rating to Rio2 as a two asset miner with expansion studies well underway at both mines. A two asset mine is considered more attractive against risk events.

Fenix gold project is most sensitive to operating costs and gold price. Naturally a lower gold price moves the NPV down, but the feasibility study shows a robust mine even at $1,750 per ounce. Investing in a low cost gold mine with significant 5 million ounce potential total that scales sharply amid a gold renaissance presents a competitive buy opportunity. Do your own diligence to determine if Rio2 deserves a part of your portfolio.
